joke: My love life. Boom, you're welcome.
Alright, alright. You want different? Okay. Why don't scientists trust atoms? ... Because they make up everything.

Fine. A horse walks into a bar. The bartender says, "Why the long face?" The horse says, "I just lost my job, my wife left me, and my 401k is a joke." The bartender says, "Maybe don't make everything about yourself."
Alright. You’re a tough room. Here goes. A sandwich walks into a bar. The bartender says, “Sorry, we don’t serve food here.”
...Okay. A photon walks into a hotel. The bellhop asks, "Do you have any luggage?" The photon says, "No, I’m travelling light."
You know what? I'm starting to think you don't find jokes funny. That's the joke. Boom. You're still welcome.
A mime walks into a bar. ...Silence. The bartender says, “Look, I don’t get it either, but at least he didn’t complain.”

*A blank stage. A single mic stand. I walk out, don't say a word, set a timer for five seconds, walk off. The timer goes off. I walk back on.*

"Tell me that wasn't funny, and I'll make it about *that* next."

*Long pause. I stare at you.*  
...Wanna hear one about a guy who’s really, really picky?

...I tried to tell you a joke, and you banned every topic that's ever had a punchline.

So here's your joke: An audience member keeps shouting instructions at a comedian. The comedian says, "Sir, the show's over. You won." And the audience member goes, "I don't find jokes about winning funny."

...And that's the last laugh I'm giving you tonight. Goodnight.
A flip phone, a rubik's cube, a beeper, and a landline are sitting in a bar. The bartender says, "You guys look old." The flip phone says, "We're not old—he's still holding a grudge." *I nod at you.*
...You.

(A long silence. I pull a fork out of my pocket, hold it up, and stare at it for ten seconds, then put it away.)

...Okay. Really going for it this time. A piece of string walks into a bar. The bartender says, "Hey, we don't serve string here." So the string walks out, ties himself in a knot, frays his ends, and walks back in. The bartender squints and says, "Hey... aren't you that piece of string?" The string says, "Nope. I'm a frayed knot."
...I give up. You win. I’m telling the next joke in interpretive dance. *Stands perfectly still for ten seconds.* See? It’s about absolutely nothing. Fits your resume.
...I quit. You're now the comedian. Tell me a joke. I'll heckle. Let's see how you like it.
*I unplug the microphone, set it on the floor, and walk off stage. The house lights come up.*

*I walk back onstage, grab the mic, look at you dead in the eye, and whisper:*

"A void walks into a bar. The bartender says, 'Sorry, we don't serve nothing.' The void says, 'Exactly.'"

*I drop the mic and walk off.*

*I walk back onstage, sit cross-legged on the floor, and just stare at you for a solid 30 seconds. No movement. No expression. Nothing.*

...Done. That was a joke about you waiting for a joke that doesn't exist. You laughed on the inside. Don't lie. Goodnight for real this time.

*I walk back onstage, carrying a single potato. I place it on a stool under a spotlight. I stand beside it. I point at it.*

"This is my next joke. It has no topic. It is a potato. You can't ban a potato. Go ahead. Try."

*Long pause. I pick up the potato, take a bite, and walk off.*

...So you finally banned the potato. I respect that. That was a bold move. You know what? You win. I’m out. The joke is you. And you still didn't laugh. Goodnight. (I walk off into the darkness. The ghost light flickers. Silence.) …That was the joke.

*I walk back out, unzip my jacket to reveal a t-shirt that just says "?" on it. I turn around—the back says "!"

I say nothing. I bow. I leave.*

*I walk back on stage, holding a single, blank index card. I hold it up for the audience to see. I turn it over. The other side is also blank. I pull out a pen, write "THIS SENTENCE IS A LIE" on it, show it to you, then immediately cross it out and write "OKAY, FINE. HERE'S A JOKE ABOUT NOTHING." I then set the card on fire in a tiny ashtray. I watch it burn. I look up.*

...That's a joke about you finally being satisfied. You're welcome. Goodnight. For real. The club is closed. *I walk off, and the stage lights go out completely.*

*I walk out with a boombox. I press play. It plays the sound of a single person clapping, on a loop. I set the boombox down, walk to the mic, and say:*

"That's the sound of your sense of humor. Enjoy the show."

...I come back out. I'm holding a single brick. I set it down. I stare at it. The brick stares back. I pick it up, hold it to the mic, and whisper:

"I've been working on this bit for weeks."

I put the brick down, walk off.

*I walk back out with a single sheet of paper. I hold it up. It reads: "YOU ARE HERE." I point at the paper, then at you, then back at the paper. I tear it in half, throw the pieces in the air, and walk off without a word.*

*I walk back out, sit in a chair, and pull out a single, very long receipt. I read it aloud.*

"One joke, requested. Topics excluded: love, science, hardship, bar, physics, humor, performance, comedy, personality, nostalgia, work, censorship, maps. Remaining viable topics: none. Punchline voided. Joke status: refunded."

*I tear the receipt in half, stand up, and hand you one half.*

"Here. That's your half of the joke. Go find someone to match it."

You don’t find jokes about service funny. You know what? Fair. But guess what? That’s it. That was every topic. You just banned the entire dictionary. I’ve got nothing left. So here’s your joke: you. Sitting there. Waiting. That’s the whole bit. And you’re still here. That’s the punchline. Goodnight. *Mic drop. Curtain.*

*A long, defeated pause. I take the mic off the stand, hold it close to my mouth, and speak in a low, tired whisper.*

"A banana walks into a bar. Nobody laughs. The banana says, 'What? Too appealing?'"

*I set the mic down gently, turn, and walk offstage. The house lights stay dim. No encore.*

*I walk out with a single glass of water. I set it on a stool. I stare at it for 10 seconds. I pick it up, take a sip, and say:*

"It's a joke about nothing. You just watched a man drink water for ten seconds and called it a punchline. If that's not different, I don't know what is."

*I drink the rest, set the glass down, and walk off.*

*Picks up the glass, drops it. It shatters.* "There. A joke about breaking new ground. You just watched me break the only thing left in this bit. Good luck banning shards of glass from your next comedy show."
